card.intuition: For each item, decide to take it or leave it — and remember the best outcome of every (item, capacity) subproblem so you never recompute.
card.analogy: Packing a hiking backpack: for each piece of gear, you choose to pack it or skip it, and you track the best value you can carry for every possible weight limit.
card.space_complexity: O(n × W), reducible to O(W) with rolling array
when_to_use: Each item can be used at most once (0/1 constraint)
Problem asks to maximize value subject to a capacity constraint
Subset-sum and partition problems (boolean variant of knapsack)
gotchas: Iterating the capacity loop backwards when using a 1D table — forward iteration would allow an item to be picked more than once (that's unbounded knapsack)
Off-by-one on table dimensions: dp has n+1 rows and W+1 columns
Fractional knapsack is NOT solved by DP — use a greedy value/weight ratio sort instead

exercise.prompt: Complete the 0/1 knapsack DP. Return the maximum value achievable within capacity W.
hints: If you take item i-1, you gain values[i-1] but must reduce available capacity by weights[i-1]
The value you had available before item i-1 at that reduced capacity is dp[i-1][w - weights[i-1]]
exercise.test_code:
r1 = knapsack([2,3,4,5],[3,4,5,7],8)
assert r1 == 10, f'Expected 10, got {r1}'
r2 = knapsack([1,2,3],[6,10,12],5)
assert r2 == 22, f'Expected 22 (take w=2+w=3), got {r2}'
r3 = knapsack([10],[100],5)
assert r3 == 0,  f'Item too heavy: expected 0, got {r3}'
r4 = knapsack([1],[1],1)
assert r4 == 1,  f'Trivial case: expected 1, got {r4}'
print('PASS')
